observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City Engineer to furnish the City Accountant of the duly signed/approved Certificate of
Completion and SWA of all completed projects as at year-end and the latter to
recognize the PPEs completed as at year-end totaling P24,845,787.71 in the General
Fund books; and
b. City Accountant to recognize those properties that are to be procured and
implemented in the future under the trust fund using the appropriate PPE accounts
in the Trust Fund books and transfer such properties to the General Fund books
upon completion pursuant to Section 104 of MNGAS for LGUs.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the:
a. City Accountant to disclose on the NTFSs the total amount of non-existing/missing
PPEs as of year-end; and
b. General Services Officer and the members of the Inventory Committee to adopt the
procedural guidelines on the conduct of one-time cleansing of PPE accounts and
prepare the PIP and submit to the Audit Team pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-
006.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to recognize the
utilization of transfers, assistance and subsidies in the appropriate asset or expense
accounts, with a corollary entry recording the related revenue in the Trust Fund books
of accounts and prepare and submit to the Audit Team the Statement of Financial
Performance of the Trust Fund.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the:
a. City Health Officer to render monthly report to the City Accounting Office on the
receipts, issuances, and balances of all kinds of vaccines, medicines and ancillary
supplies directly received from other government and private agencies; and
b. City Accountant to recognize in the books of accounts the cost of ending inventory
as of December 31, 2022 of the COVID-19 vaccines and all other vaccines, and
ancillary supplies received from the DOH and record the future receipts and
issuances of the same.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:
a. City Treasurer to prepare the list of unreleased checks as of year-end and submit
to the City Accountant within the first week of the succeeding year; and
b. City Accountant to restore to the Cash in Bank and appropriate liability account the
corresponding amount of unreleased check as of year-end in compliance with
IPSASs 2 and COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Budget Officer and City
Accountant to use the appropriate account for the appropriation and recording in the
books of accounts the other compensation and personnel benefit contributions of
casual employees in succeeding years in accordance with the prescribed Chart of
Accounts in LGUs pursuant to COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:
a. City Budget Officer to prepare and maintain the RAAO and SAAOB for Continuing
Appropriations which will be the basis for the monitoring of appropriation funded
out of continuing appropriation and submit the SAAOB to the Office of the City
Accountant before the finalization of the SCBAA; and
b. City Accountant to prepare the SCBAA based on the SAAOB submitted by the City
Budget Officer to present the correct balances of the Continuing Appropriations’
original and final budgets, and the remaining balances at year-end.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:
a. City Personnel Officer to prepare the Personnel Schedule of Casual Employees and
Plantilla of Casual Appointment using the daily wage rate computed by dividing the
corresponding monthly salary rate in the salary schedule by 22 working days
pursuant to RA 11466 and DBM LBC No. 143;
b. City Budget Officer to provide sufficient appropriation for the salaries and wages
and benefits of all casual employees based on the salary schedule provided under
RA No. 11466 and submit to the Sangguniang Panlungsod for approval; and
c. City Personnel Officer and City Accountant to pay the salaries and wages of all
casual employees at uniform percentage rates of the salary schedule similar to the
regular employees of the City Government pursuant to RA No. 11466.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:
a. City Engineer to conscientiously carry out the detailed engineering of the
subsequent projects to be implemented by the City, which include among others
the conduct of survey and site investigation, and preparation of site or right of way
acquisition plans and resettlement action plans, pursuant to 2016 Revised IRR of
RA 9184, to ensure that the same conditions/observation will not happen again; and
b. City Engineer and City Planning and Development Coordinator monitor the
implementation of all infrastructure projects and ensure its timely completion as
planned.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Assessor to prepare the Schedule
of Market Values of real properties in the City of Lipa based on the prevailing market
prices; propose the necessary revisions on the 2005 Code of Classification, Appraisal
and Assessment of Real Property in the City of Lipa; and submit to the Sangguniang
Panlungsod for appropriate action.

We also recommended that the Sangguniang Panlungsod consider, evaluate, amend
and approved the proposed revisions on the 2005 Code of Classification, Appraisal and
Assessment of Real Property in the City of Lipa, submitted by the City Assessor
through a City Ordinance, and ensure revision of real property assessments every
three years thereafter pursuant to Section 219 of RA 7160.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the Bids and Awards Committee
Secretariat to cause the submission of contracts and its supporting documents to the
Commission on Audit within five working days upon its perfection pursuant to COA
Circular No. 2009-001.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:
a. City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer, in coordination with the
CDRRMC, to prepare and submit the LDRRMFIP containing the list of programs,
projects, and activities (PPAs) and sources of funding, whether current year
appropriation and special trust funds from unexpended Local Disaster Risk
Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF) of prior years or other sources; and
b. CDRRMC to evaluate the feasibility of planned PPAs, especially those held under
Continuing Appropriations, and identify projects which will no longer be
implemented for possible reversion and reprogramming to other disaster-related
PPAs.

We recommended that the City Mayor enjoin the City Accountant and City General Services Office to identify the discrepancies between the PPE Ledger and RPCPPE balances and present the accurate balance of the PPE accounts in accordance to Paragraph 27 of PPSAS 1. In addition, create a separate Inventory Committee for one-time cleansing of PPE required under COA Circular No. 2020-006.
observations.managementAction: The Inventory Committee and General Services Office conducted the physical count of Land Improvements, Buildings and Other Structure of all PPEs, hence, identified the non-existing/missing PPEs as of December 31, 2022. Executive Order No. 6 series of 2022, creating the Lipa City Inventory Committee, for the conduct of physical count and disposition of Non-existing/Missing PPE for One-Time Cleansing pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006, was issued by the City Mayor on April 19, 2022. The Physical Inventory Plan for the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts was not yet prepared as of December 31, 2022.
observations.reason: PIP was not prepared and approved as of year-end.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Treasurer make a written request to the LBP Branch officers to provide information on the source of funds transferred to the City's accounts; City Treasurer to establish a system for online payments; City Accountant to record the collections from unknown source totaling P52,149.46 to Trust Fund in the meantime, as indicated in the letter from CTO received on March 17, 2022 to transfer said amount to Trust Fund while the verification of those payments is not yet confirmed.
observations.managementAction: Written request to the LBP, Lipa City Branch was made by the City Treasurer per letter dated April 5, 2022. The City Treasurer submitted to the City Mayor procedures for the online payments per letter dated March 24, 2022. The City Accountant recorded the collections of P52,149.46 which was reported in the Bank Reconciliation Statement as book reconciling item as of December 31, 2021 and was recorded by the City Accountant in the City's book of accounts in CY 2022.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant and City Treasurer to transfer to the General Fund the remaining balance of the General Revision Program in 2018 in the amount of P1,797,194.94 which can be used by the City to finance the conduct of the new general revision program of the City Assessor’s Office. Further recommended that the City Assessor and City Budget Officer submit a request and/or justification for the utilization of the said fund in the new general revision program to be included in the Annual Budget or Supplemental Budget, subject to approval of the Sangguniang Panlungsod.
observations.managementAction: The unutilized balance of P1,797,194.94 was transferred from the Trust Fund to the General Fund per Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) No. 2022-04-000348 dated April 21, 2022 and recorded in the General Fund book per JEV No. 2022-04-003717 dated April 25, 2022. The reverted amount was still part of the savings of the City and will be used for funding thru supplemental budget for the general revision of the schedule of fair market values of real properties, if necessary.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to ensure that future disbursements must be supported with complete documents and require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to post on the City's Website all procurement documents that required posting.
observations.managementAction: The necessary expenses identified by the City General Services Officer and City Budget Officer was incorporated in the approved Annual Budget of the City. The personnel in the Audit Division of the City Accounting Office ensures that the disbursement vouchers were supported with complete documents. The City Accounting Office submitted the lacking documents to the Audit Team.
observations.reason: The noted lacking documents such as Work Profile, Attendance Sheet/ Daily Time Records of the beneficiaries was not yet submitted by the department/implementing office to the City Accounting Office, hence not forwarded to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer to revise the LDRRMFIP of the City for CY 2021 by including the financial assistance received from National Government in CY 2020 and February 23, 2021, and submit the same to the Audit Team; require the City Engineer to submit the status of the implementation of the projects, and the City Accountant to facilitate immediate disbursement if already completed; and instruct the Local Finance Committee to prepare required monthly and other quarterly Report on Fund Utilization and Status of Program/Project Implementation and submit to NDRRMC and oversight bodies.
observations.managementAction: The revised LDRRMFIP which included the financial assistance received from National Government was submitted to the Audit Team on April 21, 2022. The status of implementation of the projects were prepared and submitted by the City Engineer and processed by the City Accountant. The City maintained coordination with oversight bodies.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Assessor declare for tax assessment purposes the noted lots without complete documents and provide the City Accounting Office the necessary information
observations.managementAction: City General Services Officer to update and provide a complete description of each lot on the Property Card based on available documents
observations.reason: if land is determined non-existing follow COA Circular No. 2020-006 procedures.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to require suppliers to submit missing documents such as ITR for CY 2019 and Astronmed to submit Mayor’s Permit for CY 2020, and generally ensure compliance with procurement documentary requirements.
observations.managementAction: The required documents were submitted to the Audit Team on May 6, 2022. The Mayor’s Permit and ITR were provided as requested.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to submit lacking documents within 60 days and require accountable officers to affix signatures on payrolls attached as supporting documents for liquidation of cash advances and submit certification that payees were paid
observations.managementAction: require the City Accountant to require concerned City officials to affix their signatures in the payrolls attached as supporting documents for the liquidation of payments for replacement beneficiaries.
observations.reason: Fully Implemented

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor ensure that planned PPAs embodied in the investment plan support disaster risk reduction activities and provide a copy of the LDRRMFIP or supplemental AIP approved by the Sangguniang Panlungsod and submit to Audit Team to avoid suspension in audit.
observations.managementAction: The LDRRMFIP for CY 2020 prepared by the CDRRMO was submitted to the Audit Team. The city ensured that PPAs were disaster risk reduction related as identified by the City officials.
observations.reason: The proof of approval by the Sangguniang Panlungsod was not submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant in coordination with PRC to submit the written notice and periodic liquidation reports within 60 days from receipt for further evaluation.
observations.managementAction: The City Accounting Office submitted the Statement of Account from PRC indicating the total number of issued kits with corresponding total amount utilized of P6,996,500.00 as of August 1, 2020. The PRC submitted liquidation reports for further evaluation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the City Mayor require concerned personnel to prepare and submit Report on Receipt and Distribution/Issuance of Donated Relief Goods, Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances and furnish a one-time report to NDRRMC; provide a plan to safeguard remaining donated used clothes against loss and wastage.
observations.managementAction: The City General Services Officer endorsed to the Audit Team the inventory of the donated items to the City Government, although not in the prescribed format, and was able to present the remaining balances as of April 28, 2021. He informed that other donations were coordinated with offices involved.
observations.reason: Report was not prepared as of year-end.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Treasurer review the accuracy of the List of Real Property Tax collectibles provided at beginning of year and harmonize with the Monthly Report of Assessment generated by the City Assessor's Office.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer provided the list of real property collectibles for CY 2022 totaling P596,774,342.24 while the City Assessor reported taxable land, buildings and other improvements totaling P34,325,264,370.00 as of Dec 31, 2021. The City Treasurer reconciled accounts and coordinated for harmonization.

observations.recommendation: We recommended City Accountant prepare and maintain subsidiary ledgers for Loans Receivable – Others accounts and regularly reconcile with general ledger and establish correct balance by validating/evaluating supporting records
observations.managementAction: ensure recording in correct accounts and prepare adjusting entries as necessary.
observations.reason: Fully Implemented

observations.recommendation: We recommended City Mayor require the City Accountant to prepare adjusting entries to reclassify affected accounts for amounts received from NGAs, GOCCs and retention fees from contractors to the appropriate accounts per IPSAS and COA Circular.
observations.managementAction: The City Accounting Office prepared necessary journal entries and reclassified amount of P1,185,400.47 out of P55,599,694.39 to affected accounts per JEVs 2022-01-000295, 2022-01-000297, and 2023-01-000321. Batangas State University and other agencies submitted supporting documents later for verification.
observations.reason: City Accounting Office is still reviewing the specific purposes of amounts received from other government agencies.

observations.recommendation: We recommended City Mayor require City Community Affairs Office to exhaust all measures to substantiate the back accounts with supporting documents and submit same to Accounting Office for recording.
observations.managementAction: Batangas State University submitted billing and supporting documents to the City Government. The City Accounting Office pre-audited the supporting documents and expected to process the initial payment in April 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended City Mayor direct BAC Secretariat to submit a copy of the contract including all documents forming part thereof to the Auditor within five working days upon perfection per COA Circular No. 2009-001.
observations.managementAction: Contracts were submitted to the Audit Team after the completion of the projects. The BAC Secretariat committed to deliver future perfected contracts on time in accordance with the deadline set.
observations.reason: Contracts were not submitted within five working days upon perfection in CY 2019

observations.recommendation: We recommended City Mayor instruct General Services Office to procure insurance coverage for all insurable buildings including those with insurable interest and submit PIF to Auditor and GSIS by April 30 each year.
observations.managementAction: The City General Services Officer submitted the PIF to the Office of the City Auditor and GSIS and insured 15 properties including the Lipa Public Market Building. Some properties remained uninsured due to appropriation constraints.
observations.reason: The appropriation was not enough to cover insurance premium for all properties

observations.recommendation: We recommended City Treasurer proceed with levy of remaining delinquent real properties through issuance of warrant of levy and institution of civil action for collection, publicly advertise properties for sale or auction and consider purchase of real property after auction as necessary to enforce collection.
observations.managementAction: As a matter of procedure, the City Treasurer delivered notices to delinquent taxpayers, coordinated fieldwork and sent notices via registered mail. Some delinquent property owners could not be located. List of delinquencies was published in newspapers and requested barangay posting. The City increased RPT delinquency collection from P59,377,021.63 in CY 2021 to P78,770,964.57 in CY 2022.
observations.reason: Some properties could not be acted upon immediately because owners could not be located and further procedures are required before advertising/auction

observations.recommendation: We recommended City Mayor require Market Committee to study amendment of Sections 5 and 8 of Market Code and draft proposal for Finance Committee
observations.managementAction: Market Superintendent and City Treasurer to collect delinquencies and enforce cancellation of lease contracts if warranted
observations.reason: The Market Committee conducted a study on existing Market Code but did not recommend revision in 2022 due to financial crisis from COVID-19. Out of total delinquencies of P1,206,515.00 recorded, P741,924.15 was collected as of Dec 31, 2022. City Accounting Office recorded unpaid market rental receivables of P1,206,515.00 and new delinquent market rent receivables for 2022 of P739,241.46.

observations.recommendation: We recommended City Mayor require City Accountant to cause immediate submission of paid DVs and their supporting documents to Auditor for verification and audit as required in Section 347 of RA 7160 and COA Circular to avoid suspension/disallowance
observations.managementAction: otherwise suspend payments of salaries of officials concerned relative to unsubmitted DVs per PD No. 1445 Section 122(2).
observations.reason: Partially Implemented

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor enjoin the City Accountant and
City General Services Officer to identify the discrepancies between the PPE Ledger
balances and RPCPPE to present the accurate balance of the PPE accounts in
accordance to Paragraph 27 of PPSAS 1. In addition, create a separate Inventory
Committee for one-time cleansing of PPE required under COA Circular No. 2020-006,
and approve the Physical Inventory Plan necessary for the conduct and complete
physical inventory.

Likewise, we recommended that the City Mayor instruct the duly created
Inventory Committee, the City General Services Office and City Accounting Office to
strictly follow the general guidelines, procedural guidelines, reconciliation of
inventory count per RPCPPE with property and accounting records, disposition
procedures for non-existing/missing PPEs, procedures in the derecognition of
non-existing/missing PPEs without available records of accountability, applicable
accounting entries and required disclosure in the Notes to the Financial Statements
required under COA Circular No. 2020-006 in the conduct of inventory taking for one-
time cleansing of PPE.

Further, we recommended that the City Mayor hire additional job order personnel, if
necessary, to be included in the members of the Inventory Committee or replace the
members of the committee who will be relieved of all their regular duties, to ensure
that the Inventory Committee will devote their full time in the conduct of the physical
inventory taking until the same is completed, and not to hamper the operations of the
City during inventory taking.

Lastly, we recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Budget Officer to
provide budgetary requirements to cover the necessary expenses in the conduct of
inventory taking.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the:

a. City Treasurer to make a written request to the LBP officers to provide
information on the source of funds transferred to the City’s account within the
purview of Philippines’ banking rules and regulations;

b. City Treasurer to establish a system for online payments, subject to the
approval of the City Mayor, where payees can be easily traced to avoid the
recurrence of the abovementioned situation, and increase the unreconciled
amount from online payments; and

c. City Accountant to record the collections from unknown source totaling
P52,149.46 to Trust Fund in the meantime, as indicated in the letter from CTO
received on March 17, 2022 to transfer said amount to Trust Fund while the
verification of those payments is not yet confirmed.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant and City Treasurer
to transfer to the General Fund the remaining balance of the General Revision
Program in CY 2018 in the amount of P1,797,194.94 which can be used by the City to
finance the conduct of the new general revision of assessment and property
classification. Relative thereto, we also recommended that the City Mayor instruct the
City Assessor and City Budget Officer to submit a request and/or justification for the
utilization of said fund in the new general revision program of the City, if necessary,
by the City Assessor’s Office to be included in the Annual Budget or Supplemental
Budget, subject to the approval of the Sangguniang Panlungsod.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant and Bids and
Awards Committee (BAC) to submit the lacking documents noted above, or provide
justification for further evaluation of the Audit Team, and require the BAC to post on
the Lipa City’s Website all related procurement documents that required to be posted.
Likewise, we recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to
ensure that future disbursements must be supported with complete required
documents.

We recommended that the City Mayor:

a. require the City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer to revise the
LDRRMFIP of the City for CY 2021 by including the financial assistance
received from National Government in CY 2020 and February 23, 2021, and
submit the same to the Audit Team;

b. require the City Engineer to submit the status of the implementation of the
projects, and the City Accountant to facilitate immediate disbursement of the
financial assistance received if already completed, subject to usual
accounting and auditing rules and regulations; and

c. instruct the Local Finance Committee, namely, the City Accountant,
City Budget Officer, City Engineer, City Treasurer and City Planning and
Development Coordinator, to prepare the required monthly and other quarterly
Report on Fund Utilization and Status of Program/Project/Activity
Implementation, and submit the same to the NDRRM Council and other
oversight bodies, copy furnished the Audit Team.

We recommended that the City Mayor require:
a) the City Accountant to prepare and maintain property ledger cards for Local Road Networks and Road Networks. disclose the for the prescribed format as provided under COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 25, 2015. Xxx
observations.managementAction: The management claimed Not Property ledger cards were not prepared and maintained by the City Accounting Office since the latter is currently waiting for the submission by the City Engineering Office.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the BAC and Technical Working Group (TWG) to: b) submit their basis for rating the legal, technical and financial documents submitted by bidders as “Passed” during the preliminary evaluation and post-qualification for procurements. d) impose administrative sanctions/penalties to the contractors/suppliers that submission of eligibility requirements containing false information or falsified documents.
observations.managementAction: The Management stated that the matter has been referred to the Office of the City Legal Office and BAC Chairperson. The BAC Secretariat requested for at least 30 working days to consolidate all the documents and other requisites noted in this observation for submission in order for them to make an exhaustive response to the observation.
observations.reason: The City have yet to submit the basis for rating the legal, technical and financial documents submitted by bidders as “Passed” during the preliminary evaluation and post-qualification for procurements. No action taken to impose administrative sanctions/penalties to contractors/suppliers that submitted eligibility requirements containing false information or falsified documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the BAC to strictly adhere with the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and other related rules and regulations especially on the completeness of documents in each stage of the bidding/procurement process. Also the BAC Secretariat in coordination with the City Accountant to submit the required supporting documents for audit purposes and to prevent the issuance of Notice of Suspension/Disallowance.
observations.managementAction: The Management remarked that starting 2018, the City refrained from procuring janitorial services for Ospital ng Lipa and instead the services were provided by the job order personnel hired by the LGU, while supplies used were sourced from regular appropriation for other supplies. The BAC requested for at least 30 working days to consolidate all the documents and other requisites noted in this observation for submission.
observations.reason: The required supporting documents remained unsubmitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the BAC and TWG to submit the above documents for further evaluation of the CY 2017 procurements, and strictly adhere with Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and other related rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction: No action taken.
observations.reason: The BAC still failed to submit the required documents regarding procurement.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require: a) the City Assessor to declare for tax assessment the unrecorded lots based on the Tax Declarations; b) the City Accountant to recognize as assets the noted unrecorded lots and make appropriate adjustments on the double recording of lots; and c) the General Services Officer to facilitate the appraisal of the unrecorded lots to determine the fair market value to be recorded and exhaust all possible means to document the legality of ownership of the lots through a Transfer Certificate of Title (TCT).
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant sent letter to the BAC requesting for an update on the status of compliance with the audit recommendations.
observations.reason: No action taken. The City did not submit evidence that the audit recommendation was implemented.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Administrator to formulate guidelines for the sponsorship grants to harmonize with DBM Circular Letter No. 2008-8 and DSWD Administrative Order No. 5, series of 2008 to ensure equitable allocation of assistance to constituents and economical use of City funds.
observations.managementAction: The City Administrator referred the matter to the Office of the City Legal Officer.
observations.reason: The Management failed to present guidelines for sponsorship grants to harmonize with DBM and DSWD.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor make representation with the Sangguniang Panlungsod for the creation and installation of the IAS directly under the Office of the City Mayor, in order to improve and strengthen the internal control systems for an efficient and effective fiscal administration and performance of the City Government.
observations.managementAction: No action taken. The creation and installation of the IAS directly under the Office of the City Mayor was not implemented by the City Government.
observations.reason: No action taken; IAS not implemented.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor require:

a) the Inventory Committee, City Accounting Office and CGSO to reconcile the
RPCPPE with the accounting and property records and identify the details of
the discrepancies by working back on prior years’ transactions, and prepare
the adjusting entries, if necessary;

b) the General Services Officer (GSO) to prepare corresponding Inventory and
Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property for the identified unserviceable
properties and initiate the process of disposing the same in accordance with
Section 79 of PD No. 1445 and the rules provided in COA Circular
No. 92-386;

c) the GSO to submit the necessary documents, together with the Official
Receipt to the Accounting Office, after the disposal, as the basis of the latter to make the necessary accounting entry regarding the disposal of the unserviceable properties;

d) both City Accounting Office and CGSO to perform the necessary procedures
to specifically identify the cost of properties reported under lump sum
amount in the Lapsing Schedule for proper determination/computation of depreciation;

e) the GSO to maintain efficient property management by following the
prescribed procedures in property recording and maintain accurate property
records and regularly renew PAR; and

f) the Supply Officer to assign property tag/number for each PPE during the
physical inventory to easily identify and trace the location of the said PPE.

We recommended that the City Mayor require:

a) the City Accountant to ensure that transactions are recognized at the proper
accounting period to achieve the fair presentation of accounts in the
financial statements;

b) the City Treasurer to review the accuracy of the List of Real Property Tax
Collectibles provided at the beginning of the year and harmonize the same
with the Monthly Report of Assessment as of year-end or assessment roll
generated by the City Assessor’s Office; and

c) the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to reconcile the balance of RPT
and SET Receivables as reported in the City’s books of accounts and
List of Delinquent Taxpayer as of December 31, 2019 and effect the
necessary adjustments.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to:

a) prepare and maintain subsidiary ledgers for Loans Receivable-Others
accounts and regularly reconcile with the general ledger balances and update
recording/posting of transactions at all times in compliance with Section 10 of
the MNGAS and Section Nos. 111, 112 and 114 of PD No. 1445; and

b) establish the correct balance of the total Loans Receivable-Others account by
validating/evaluating the related transactions with the records maintained by
the CSWDO and City Cooperative Officer such as contracts and supporting
documents on the collections received by the CTO, and to prepare adjusting
entries, as necessary.

We recommended that City Mayor require the City Accountant to record its financial
transactions conformably with Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1 and in accordance with COA
Circular No. 2015-009. Moreover, require the City Accountant to prepare the adjusting
entries to reclassify the affected accounts for the amounts received from NGAs,
GOCCs and retention fee from the contractors in the proper/appropriate accounts.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant and the
City Community Affairs Office to verify and provide, respectively, all pertinent records
for the scholarship assistance granted to the students on the said period and set up
the appropriate amount of accounts payable to BatStateU by drawing a necessary
journal entry. If possible, require the City Accountant and the City Community Affairs
Officer to make representation with the University’s counterpart officials to discuss
the matters relative to the retrieval and reconciliation of their respective records.

We also recommended that the City Mayor require the City Community Affairs Office
to exhaust all possible measures to substantiate the back accounts with
corresponding supporting documents and submit the same to the Accounting Office
for recording purposes.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant:

a) to refrain from recording all of the fines and penalties charged in relation to the
collection of property taxes to the RPT- Basic and SET accounts and credit the
same to the Tax Revenue - Fines and Penalties - Property Taxes account in
accordance with COA Circular No. 2015-009; and

b) to ensure that revenue from real property taxes are recognized at the proper
accounting period to achieve the fair presentation of revenue account in the
financial statements.

We reiterated our previous audit recommendations that the City Mayor instruct:

a) Disbursing Officer to ensure that all cash advances to be drawn are supported
with payrolls or list of payees with net payments;

b) Disbursing Officer to liquidate the cash advance within five days after each 15
day/end month pay period pursuant to Item 5.1.1. of COA Circular No. 97-002;
and

c) concerned personnel to prepare a schedule of payments of PS and MOOE
wherein the payees can receive their cash on time and the Disbursing Officers
can liquidate their cash advances within the period specified in the
aforementioned COA Circular. If the payee will not be able to receive the cash
within the time frame of liquidation, require the concerned payee to execute
Special Power of Attorney to authorize someone to receive the payment on
his/her behalf. Likewise, inform the payees of the schedule of payments to
avoid refund of the cash advance.

We likewise recommended that the City Mayor designate additional Disbursing
Officer to cater the need for various cash advances for the period.

Further, it is recommended that Management consider the automation of the payment
of salaries and wages of casual and job order employees using bank services to save
time, effort and manpower services.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct:
• the BAC to -
a) adopt the latest Philippine Bidding Documents issued by the
GPPB in the preparation of the bidding documents;
b) in the conduct of bid evaluation during Bid Opening, strictly comply with
Sections 30.1 and 30.2 of the 2016 IRR of RA No. 9184, which is to open
the first envelope containing eligibility and technical requirements of all
bidders before opening the second envelope containing financial proposal
of all bidders who were rated “passed” using non-discretionary “pass/fail”
criterion;
c) include in the Minutes of Bid Opening a categorical statement that the
BAC checked the submitted documents of each bidder against a checklist
of required documents to ascertain if they are present, using a
non-discretionary “pass/fail” criterion, as stated in the Instruction to
Bidders;
d) ensure that the submitted SLCC similar to the contract to be bid must be
at least 50 per cent of the ABC, provided, however, that contractors under
Small A and B categories without similar experience on the contract to be
bid may be allowed to bid if the cost of such contract is not more than the
ARCC of their registration based on the guidelines as prescribed by
Philippine Contractors Accreditation Board;
e) ensure that the submitted Mayor’s Permit, Certificate of Philippine
Government Electronic Procurement System (PhilGEPS) Registration and
Tax Clearance is valid and not expired during Bid Opening;
f) ensure that there are no discrepancies in the submitted bid such as, but
not limited to, the List of Key Personnel and List of Equipment assigned
and pledged to the proposed contract;
g) in the preparation of Abstract of Bid as Evaluated, ensure that there are
detailed mathematical and arithmetic computations in determining the
170
Lowest Calculated Bid (LCB);
h) ensure that the Construction Safety and Health Program is approved by the
DOLE;
• the Technical Working Group (TWG) to submit comprehensive Post-Qualification
Evaluation Report showing proof of validation of submitted legal, technical and
financial documents such as, but not limited to, the following:
1. The authenticity of the Mayor’s Permit from the issuing local
government unit;
2. Printout of Bureau of Internal Revenue (BIR) Released Tax Clearance from
the website of the BIR showing the name of the bidder with LCB;
3. Copy of validated Securities and Exchange Commission or Department of
Trade and Industry Certificate from issuing government agency;
4. List of “blacklisted” entities and contractor issued by the GPPB;
5. Validation on the competence and experience of key personnel assigned in
the project;
6. Existence and availability of the equipment pledged in the project;
7. Existence and quality of the project submitted as SLCC; and
8. Validation on the financial capacity of the winning bidder to ensure that the
latter can sustain the operating cash flow of the transaction
• the BAC Secretariat to –
a. post the Notice of Award and Notice to Proceed in the PhilGEPS, the
website of the Procuring Entity, if any, and any conspicuous places in the
premises of the Procuring Entity within three calendar days after its
issuance to ensure the transparency in the procurement process; and
b. submit a copy of contract including each and all the documents forming
part thereof by reference of incorporation, to the Auditor within five
working days from the execution of the said contract pursuant to COA
Circular No. 2009-001.
Likewise, we recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Engineer to ensure that
the Variation Order shall be supported by original plans showing the affected portions of
the project, complete set of approved revise plans/drawings as well as the technical
specification and justification on the necessity of such variation order.
Further, we recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to require the
submission of the following documentary requirements before certifying as to the
completeness of the documents relative to the advance/partial/final payment of the
infrastructure projects, such as:
1. A written request from the Contractor, to be submitted as a contract
document, for an advance payment not exceeding 15 per cent of the total
contract price;
2. Submission and acceptance by the procuring entity of an irrevocable
standby letter of credit of equivalent value from a commercial bank, a bank
guarantee or a surety bond callable upon demand, issued by a surety or
insurance company duly licensed by the Insurance Commission and
confirmed by procuring entity;
3. Progress Billing of the contractor;
4. Letter request from the contractor of progress payments;
5. Result of applicable Test Analysis;
6. Statement of Time Elapsed;
7. Monthly Certificate of Payment corresponding the amount deducted from
periodic progress payment as payment of 15 per cent mobilization fee a
percentage equal to the percentage of the total contract price used for the
advance payment;
8. Pictures before, during and after the construction of items of work
specially the embedded items; and
171
9. As-Built Plans.
Lastly, we recommended that concerned personnel submit the lacking documents
noted, for further evaluation by the Audit Team, and the City Accountant ensure that
all disbursements of the City Government should be supported with complete
documentation to avoid suspension and/or disallowance in the audit.

We recommended that the City Mayor:

a. instruct the City Treasurer to secure authority from the LSB to support the
placement of SEF idle funds in time deposits, otherwise, instruct the
City Treasurer to transfer to appropriate SEF current account the
corresponding amount that was placed in time deposit;

b. instruct the City Treasurer to make representation with the concerned banks
to transfer/credit the interest earned on SEF special savings deposit account
to SEF current account and declare the same as additional cash available for
SEF which will be included on the next budget preparation; and

c. instruct the City Accountant to prepare necessary adjusting entry to record the
interest of SEF time deposits accounts to its corresponding books of
accounts.

We also recommended the LSB to consider in appropriating the identified SEF idle
funds in the next year’s budget deliberation to address and prioritize the noted
shortages on facilities in accordance with the guidelines in the utilization of SEF.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the:

a) GSO to procure insurance coverage for all insurable government buildings to
guarantee protection from fire, earthquake, storm and/or other acts of nature and to
ensure that all City’s assets with insurable interest are included in the PIF and submit
the same to the Office of the Auditor and GSIS not later than April 30 each year; and

b) City Budget Officer to include in the City’s annual budget the amount of
premiums for the general insurance covering all insurable properties.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Treasurer to strictly comply
with the requirement under COA Circular No. 92-389 by stamping “PAID” on the DVs
for all paid transactions to maintain a sound internal control in the disbursement of
government funds. The perforation of documents shall not be limited only to the DVs
but should include all supporting documents to avoid their reuse. A stamp
(size 2” x 3”) to be made shall provide a space for the check number issued and date
of actual payment and said spaces should be properly filled out.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:
Inventory Committee to start the physical count of all Inventory items for the General Fund (GF) and Special Education Fund (SEF) at an earlier date or before the end of each prescribed period to give the members an ample time to prepare the Report on Physical Count of Inventory (RPCI) in proper form by type and submit the same to the Auditor’s Office with the certification and approval of the Agency Head not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year.
observations.managementAction: Report on Physical Count of Inventories for the first semester of CY 2019 was submitted to the Audit Team only on July 25, 2019.
observations.reason: The City Inventory Committee failed to submit the RPCI for the second semester of CY 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to ensure that all cash advances to be drawn are supported with payrolls and payrolls or list of payees with their net payments, and to stop the granting of simultaneous cash advances to the same DO with different purpose in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002. Also to automate payment of salaries and wages of casual and job order employees.
observations.managementAction: Observation on Cash Advances was reiterated with modifications under Observation No. 7, Part II of this report.
observations.reason: Not all cash advance disbursements were supported by payrolls.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor strictly comply with the pertinent provisions of JC No. 2015-01 of the DBM, DILG, DND, GCG and COA on the time frame, use of Confidential Funds. He shall require the City Accountant to: a) state on the face of the Disbursement Voucher (DV) the period covered of the cash advance drawn and the previous cash advance for the same purpose, project or activity given to the Special Disbursing Officer requesting cash advance has been liquidated and proper accounting thereof. b) submit to the Audit Team, for review and verification, the DV for the grant of cash advance for confidential expense, together with all the required and proper supporting documents, within the prescribed period of seven days upon release of checks, pursuant to the above regulation.
observations.managementAction: DV and its supporting documents were submitted to the COA office within 2 days from the release of check last March 25, 2019.
observations.reason: There was no certification of the City Accountant stamped or printed on the DV that the period covered of the cash advance drawn for CY 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Administrator to provide/submit legal basis on the grant of additional allowance/honorarium to the aforecited barangay employees, otherwise, these will be disallowed in audit.
observations.managementAction: The Former City Administrator forwarded the City official his reply letter and stated that the City Government of Lipa is clothed with authority to provide additional incentives to different barangay functionaries as provided under Section 393 (a) of the Local Government Code of 1991 and such power is a statutory grant.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to cause the immediate submission of the remaining unsubmitted paid DVs and their supporting documents to the Auditor for verification and audit as required in Section 347 of RA No. 7160 and Section 7.2.1.a of COA Circular No. 2009-006 to avoid the issuance of notices of suspension and/or disallowance in audit on the subject transactions. Otherwise, cause the suspension of payment of the salaries of the officials concerned relative to the unsubmitted DVs, pursuant to Section 122(2) of PD No. 1445.
observations.managementAction: Out of the 403 DVs, only 22 vouchers for CY 2018 remained unsubmitted to the Audit Team. The City Accountant already sent letter to the City Treasurer requesting for follow-up the status of the said DVs for CY 2018.
observations.reason: The 22 unsubmitted DVs were not yet forwarded by the City Treasurer to the City Accountant for transmittal to the Office of the City Auditor.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require: a) the City Accountant and City Budget Officer to make the necessary reversion of the unexpended balance of LDRRMF which remained unutilized for five years or more from Trust Fund to GF; and b) require the CDRRMO to abide with Items 5.1.5 and 5.1.6 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 regarding reporting standard of the LDRRMFIP and Report of Utilization of LDRRMF.
observations.managementAction: The City Government already reverted back Unutilized Quick Response and Mitigation Fund for CY 2012-2013 from Trust Fund to Unappropriated Surplus of the GF with DV and Journal Entry Voucher dated April 3, 2019. The CDRRMO complied with requirements and the transfer station has been rehabilitated and waste processing machines are now installed and fully operational.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to reconcile the balances as of December 31, 2018 of RPT and SET Receivables and their reciprocal accounts with total delinquency tax as reported by the City Treasurer and effect the necessary adjustments; and the City Treasurer to facilitate the preparation and submission of the duly certified list showing the names of all taxpayers and the tax due from each to the City Accountant.
observations.managementAction: The CTO submitted the duly certified list showing the names of all taxpayers and the tax due from each as required by the City Accountant.
observations.reason: The balances of RPT and SET Receivables and their reciprocal accounts were not reconciled with the reported List of Delinquent Taxpayer as of December 31, 2019 from City Treasurer’s Office (CTO). Observation on RPT and SET Receivables was reiterated with modifications under Observation No. 2, Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Treasurer: a) proceed to the levy of the remaining 31,875 real properties with delinquent taxes through issuance of a warrant on or before, or simultaneously with, the institution of the civil action for the collection of the delinquent tax; b) publicly advertise for sale or auction the 453 real properties or a usable portion thereof which are already under Warrant of Levy but remain unpaid; and c) exercise the sale through auction and purchase of real property for want of bidder for other properties after the issuance of Warrant of Levy became futile, as may be necessary to enforce collection of delinquent real property taxes.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurers Office started issuing warrant of levy last November 2019. For CY 2020, due to the prevailing economic condition after the eruption of Taal Volcano and COVID 19 pandemic community quarantine, the implementation of the audit recommendations were suspended.
observations.reason: Scheduled to proceed to the next step of institution of civil actions for CY 2020. Implementation was suspended due to Taal volcano eruption and COVID-19 pandemic.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous audit recommendations that the City Mayor instruct the officials concerned to: 1. ensure that all cash advances to be drawn are supported with payrolls and payrolls or list of payees with their net payments; 2. stop the granting of simultaneous cash advances to the same DO even if with different purpose; 3. ensure that cash advances are granted for a specific purpose only and not in anticipation of expenditures to be made during the month; 4. designate additional DO to cater the need for various cash advances for the period; and 5. automate the payment of salaries and wages of casual and job order employees using bank services to save time and manpower.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant and CTO noted the observations and stated they will comply; City requested additional plantilla positions and initiated contact with LBP regarding automation.
observations.reason: Some grants of cash advances were supported by payrolls. The CTO is still waiting for response from LBP; the implementation on automation is pending.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to: a) state on the face of the DV the period covered of the cash advance drawn and the previous cash advance for the same purpose and b) submit to the Audit Team, for review and verification, the DV for the grant of cash advance for confidential expense together with all required supporting documents within seven days upon release of checks.
observations.managementAction: DV and its supporting documents were submitted to COA within 2 days from release of check March 25, 2019. The City Accountant issued a separate certification dated March 25, 2019.
observations.reason: There was no certification of the City Accountant stamped or printed on the DV that the period covered of the cash advance drawn for CY 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor through the City Solid Waste Management Board (SWMB) direct the City Environment and Natural Resources Officer (CENRO) to strictly implement the waste segregation and volume reduction at source through: a) activation of the Barangay Solid Waste Management Committees; b) intensifying information dissemination campaign; c) strict implementation of 'no segregation, no collection' rule; d) provision of properly labeled trash bins; and e) firm and consistent enforcement of ordinances regarding waste disposal and imposition of fines. Also require the City Mayor to instruct concerned barangay officials to establish and operate their MRFs and consider clustered MRFs; and direct CENRO to rebuild and improve the waste processing facilities.
observations.managementAction: The City Mayor issued EO No. 32 s. 2019 enjoining segregation, conducted orientations and letters to barangays; transfer station rehabilitated and waste processing machines installed; CENRO implemented various measures and projects like 'A Trash to Cash'.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to cause the immediate submission of the remaining unsubmitted paid DVs and their supporting documents to the Auditor for verification and audit as required to avoid issuance of notices of suspension/disallowance. Otherwise, suspend payment of salaries of officials concerned.
observations.managementAction: Out of the 403 DVs, only 22 vouchers for CY 2018 remained unsubmitted. The City Accountant sent letter to the City Treasurer requesting follow-up on status. The City received some of the DVs and is in process.
observations.reason: 22 unsubmitted DVs remain outstanding and not yet forwarded by City Treasurer.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor follow strictly the above provisions of law on all of the submission of the documents and for the BAC to strictly adhere with the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and other related rules and regulations especially on the completeness of documents in each stage of the bidding/procurement process.
observations.managementAction: The BAC to ensure that withheld retention money on every progress payment reached three months and supplier performed satisfactorily was implemented. The BAC Secretariat also noted that they will submit documents for further evaluation.
observations.reason: No documents were submitted to the Audit Team. For CY 2018, the retention documents were not submitted for audit.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor require:

a. the City Accountant to reconcile the balances as of December 31, 2018 of RPT and
SET Receivables and their reciprocal accounts with total delinquency tax as
reported by the City Treasurer and effect the necessary adjustments; and
b. the City Treasurer to facilitate the preparation and submission of the duly certified
list showing the names of all taxpayers and the tax due from each at the start of
the year to the City Accountant as a basis of recording in the books of accounts
the RPT/SET Receivables and the equivalent Deferred Income accounts.

We recommended that the City Mayor require:

a. the City Assessor to declare for tax assessment purposes the noted lots without
Tax Declarations;
b. the City Accountant to recognize as assets the noted unrecorded lots based on
the TCT/OCT or Tax Declarations and make appropriate adjustments on the
double recording of lots; and
c. the General Services Officer to facilitate the appraisal of the unrecorded lots to
determine the fair market value to be recorded and exhaust all possible means to
document the legality of ownership of the lots through a Transfer Certificate of
Title (TCT).

We recommended that the City Treasurer:

a. proceed to the levy of the remaining 31,875 real properties with delinquent taxes
through issuance of a warrant on or before, or simultaneously with, the institution
of the civil action for the collection of the delinquent tax;
b. publicly advertise for sale or auction the 453 real properties or a usable portion
thereof which are already under Warrant of Levy but remain unpaid; and
c. exercise the sale through auction and purchase of real property for want of bidder
for other properties after the issuance of Warranty of Levy became futile, as may
be necessary to enforce collection of delinquent real property taxes.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Administrator to formulate
guidelines for the sponsorship grants to harmonize with DBM Circular Letter No.
2008-8 and DSWD AO No. 5, series of 2008 to ensure equitable allocation of
assistance to constituents and economical use of City funds.

We reiterated our previous audit recommendations that the City Mayor instruct the
officials concerned:
1. to ensure that all cash advances to be drawn are supported with payrolls or
disbursement voucher with net payments;
2. to stop the granting of simultaneous cash advances to the same Disbursing
Officer even if with different purpose in compliance with the aforecited
regulations;
3. to ensure that cash advances are granted for specific purpose only or when
service are already rendered and not in anticipation of expenditures to be made
during the month;
4. to designate additional Disbursing Officer to cater the need for various cash
advances for the period; and
5. to automate the payment of salaries and wages of casual and job order employees
using bank services to save time, effort and manpower services.

We recommended that the City Mayor strictly comply with the pertinent provisions of
Joint Circular No. 2015-01 of the DBM, DILG, DND, GCG and COA on the use of
Confidential Funds. He shall require the City Accountant to:

a. state on the face of the DV the period covered of the cash advance drawn and the
required certification as mentioned on Section 6.1.5; and
b. submit to the Audit Team, for review and verification, the disbursement voucher
for the grant of cash advance for confidential expense, together with all the
required and proper supporting documents, within the prescribed period of seven
days upon release of checks, pursuant to the above regulation.

We recommended that the City Mayor require:

a. the Market Committee to make a thorough study for the possible amendment of
Sections 5 and 8 of the Market Code and draft a proposal for deliberation of the
Finance Committee for eventual approval of the Chief Executive and concurrence
of the Sangguniang Panlungsod thru City Ordinance;
b. the Market Superintendent and City Treasurer to immediately collect
delinquencies as well as of any applicable penalties. Implement automatic
cancellation of lease contracts, if warranted;
c. the City Accountant to record receivables with probability of collection from
unpaid rental fees and the related income thereon as of December 31, 2018; and
d. the Market Superintendent to submit report of uncollected rental fees as of every
year-end to the City Accountant for proper recording and monitoring as well as for
the appropriate disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements.

We recommended that the City Mayor make representation with the Sangguniang
Panlungsod for the creation and installation of the IAS directly under the Office of the
City Mayor, in compliance with AO Nos. 278 and 70 in order to improve and
strengthen the internal control systems for an efficient and effective fiscal
administration and performance of the City Government.

We recommended that the City Mayor through the City Solid Waste Management
Board (SWMB) direct the CENRO to strictly implement the waste segregation and
volume reduction at source in the whole City of Lipa through:

a. activation of the Barangay Solid Waste Management Committees (SWMC) for
more rigorous imposition and monitoring of proper waste segregation in the
household and business establishments;
b. continuously intensifying information dissemination campaign through
coordination with the Barangay SWMC, Department of Education and other private
sectors;
c. strict implementation of “no segregation, no collection rule” on barangays where
no Material Recovery Facility (MRF) are established in the meantime specifically
on the urban areas;
d. provision of properly labeled trash bins on different offices of the City
Government and large containers on its collection area; and
e. firm and consistent enforcement of City General Ordinance No. II and other City
ordinances regarding waste disposal and imposition of appropriate fines and
penalties to offenders.

We recommended that the City Mayor through the City SWMB instruct the concerned
barangay officials to establish and operate their Materials Recovery Facility (MRF)
and consider clustered MRFs in cases of limited space in barangays. Otherwise,
appropriate sanction shall be enforced as provided by Section 50 of the same law.

Also, direct the CENRO to rebuild and improve the condition of the MRF and transfer
station of the City Government for safe, effective and efficient waste management.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to cause the
immediate submission of the remaining unsubmitted paid DVs and their supporting
documents to the Auditor for verification and audit as required in Section 347 of
Republic Act No. 7160 and Section 7.2.1.a of COA Circular No. 2009-006 to avoid the
issuance of notices of suspension and/or disallowance in audit on the subject
transactions. Otherwise, cause the suspension of payment of the salaries of the
officials concerned relative to the unsubmitted DVs, pursuant to Section 122(2) of
PD No. 1445.

We recommended that the City Mayor:

a. require the CDRRMO to ensure that the LDRRMFIP contains the details of the
projects and activities to be funded including those planned for the utilization of
the previous year’s LDRRMF, as required under Items 5.1.2 and 5.1.15 of COA
Circular 2012-002;
b. require the City Accountant and City Budget Officer to make the necessary
reversion of the unexpended balance of LDRRMF which remained unutilized aged
five years or more from Trust Fund to General Fund; and
c. require the CDRRMO to abide with Items 5.1.5 and 5.1.6 of COA Circular 2012-002
regarding reporting standard of the LDRRMFIP and Report of Utilization of
LDRRMF.

We recommended that the City Mayor require: a) the Accounting Office to carefully evaluate and properly classify PPE items into its proper account classification in accordance with the revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units (LGUs) as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015; b) the City GSO to prepare and submit to the Disposal/Appraisal Committee a duly accomplished Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) and the Waste Material Report together with the other documentary requirements to facilitate the determination of the appraised value and mode of disposal of the unserviceable properties; and c) the Accounting Office to submit to COA the Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) and other supporting documents used as basis of adjustments/reclassifications made on the PPE amounting to P341,965,966.42.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant in her letter dated May 28, 2018, duly received by the Audit Team on May 30, 2018, provided justification and reconciliation on the noted discrepancies.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the: a) General Services Officer (GSO) to prepare and maintain Road Networks property cards; b) City Engineer to provide the City Accountant and the GSO with the Road Networks complete description and estimated amount for road projects; and c) City Accountant to maintain property ledger cards for Local Road Networks and disclose the information for the said property in the Notes to Financial Statements in accordance with the prescribed format as provided under COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 25, 2015.
observations.managementAction: The General Services Officer showed Road Networks property cards. The City Engineer forwarded the City Accountant and the Accountant with the Road Networks Inventory property.
observations.reason: Concrete details were not yet determined and finalized with the City Engineering Office (CEO). Details are yet to be finalized by the CEO.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require: a) Inventory Report on Physical Count Committee to start the physical count and submit the Report of Physical Inventories for CY 2018 and submit the same to the Audit Team; b) City Veterinary Office and General Services Office to coordinate with the Accounting Office for the proper reclassification of the Veterinary Supplies and Medicines account; c) Accounting Unit and the GSO to maintain supplies ledger cards and stock cards, respectively, for each inventory item/stock; d) Accounting Unit to take up all currently purchases of items to Inventory account and record the issuance based on Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) only; e) Accountant and the GSO to conduct periodic reconciliation of their records and prepare adjustment of the affected accounts, if necessary; and f) Community Social Welfare and Development through Office/City Accounting Office to submit an explanation on why there was an inventory for Welfare Goods for Distribution when there was no known calamity that struck the City or Sangguniang Panlungsod Resolution declaring the City in the State of Calamity for CY 2017.
observations.managementAction: The City Veterinary Office and General Services Office already coordinated with the Accounting Office which duly made the necessary reclassification. The Accounting Office and the GSO are now maintaining supplies ledger cards and ledgers cards, respectively, for the subject inventory accounts. The Accounting Office is taking up all purchases of items to Inventory account and record issuances per SSMI. The City Accountant provided justification on the stockpiling of basic emergency supplies as per Section 5.2.11 of NDRRMC-DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1.
observations.reason: Inventory and inspection of unserviceable items takes time and accomplishing all accounts cannot be reasonably accomplished by year-end.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to ensure that all cash advances to be drawn are supported with payrolls and disbursement vouchers and that no cash advances shall be given unless for a legally specific purpose; that additional cash advances shall not be allowed to any official or employee unless the previous cash advance given to him is first settled or a proper accounting thereof is made; and that cash advances shall be limited to payroll or net amount of the payroll for a pay period and be supported by payroll or list of payees with their payments.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer stated they usually draw cash advances based on audited payrolls except for casual employees and job order personnel due to humanitarian consideration. He noted consideration of provision of at least five days cut off on casual employees and job order personnel services and proposal for the creation of two additional disbursing officers at their Cash Division. They inquired with depository banks regarding automation of payment of salaries and wages of casual and job order employees.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to see to it that all Obligations incurred in CY 2018 by the City Government were properly taken up in the accounts of the year they were incurred, pursuant to Section 112 of PD No. 1445 and Section 4(a) of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.
observations.managementAction: Obligations incurred in CY 2018 by the City Government were properly charged on the account of that year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct: a) the concerned officials to consolidate in one office the handling and selection of City scholars to avoid the availment of more than one scholarship and to focus on the less privileged; b) the concerned City officials to evaluate the scholarship programs and determine the students to refund amounts if they received more than one financial assistance; c) the Accounting Office to use the Scholarship account instead of Donation account in recording transactions pertaining to the grant of educational assistance; d) the respective Office to transfer preparation of payroll for Sagip Eskwela and Educational Financial Assistance Program to the Accounting Office; e) the concerned Office to revisit Implementing Guidelines and make necessary amendment on the amount being granted for Sagip Eskwela; f) the City Community Affairs Office to submit documents to support the grant of MAS College Scholarship for Indigents; g) the concerned Office to refrain from paying financial assistance to post graduate students and other students who can afford to support their studies; h) the concerned Office to revisit guidelines set for the grant of Financial Assistance to College Students; i) the members of the Sangguniang Panlungsod who represented the City in the MOA with grantees to desist from entering into such agreements in behalf of the City; and j) the stoppage of granting scholarship/financial assistance to prestigious/private universities and give priority to the less privileged students.
observations.managementAction: The City Community Affairs Office (CCAO). A single Scholarship Program for different kind of scholarship was then conceptualized. The Accounting Office used the Scholarship account instead of Donation account in recording the grant of educational assistance. The new guidelines provide P3,000.00 as grant. MAS College documents to support the grant of MAS College Scholarship for Indigents were already complied after issuance of annual audit report. The City continued the granting of financial assistance to graduate and post graduate students but now limits the amount. The City is in the process for payroll handling and pre-audit.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the BAC and TWG to: a) explain in writing why they shall not be held liable for their failure to conduct thorough evaluation of the legal, technical and financial documents submitted by bidders during post-qualification proceedings; b) submit their basis for rating the legal, technical and financial documents submitted by bidders during preliminary evaluation and post-qualification; c) be more cautious in evaluating the legal, technical and financial documents submitted by bidders during preliminary evaluation and post-qualification before recommending the award of the contract; and d) impose administrative sanctions/penalties to the contractors/suppliers for the submission of eligibility requirements containing false information or falsified documents.
observations.managementAction: The BAC Secretariat, in his letter dated June 26, 2018, duly noted by CGSO, one of the BAC members alleged that it was in good faith on their part that they certified the subject documents as “Passed”. The BAC-TWG complied with the requirements in the evaluation of legal, technical and financial documents. The BAC Secretariat stressed out that for the imposition of sanctions/penalties, they will refer them to the higher authority.
observations.reason: Not all supporting documents were submitted; the required supporting documents remained unsubmitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the: a) City Accountant, City Budget Officer, and other local officials concerned to strictly comply with Item 6.1.5 of the Joint Circular No. 2015-01 regarding limitation of cash advances to three months; b) City Accountant to submit to the Audit Team, for review and verification, the disbursement voucher for the grant of cash advance for confidential expense, together with all the required and proper supporting documents, within the prescribed period of seven days upon release of checks; c) City Budget Officer to ensure that appropriation for Confidential Expenses should be stated as such and not for Confidential/Intelligence Expense to avoid confusion; d) City Accountant to provide clarifications on the period covered of the cash advance drawn and the reason thereof; and reiterated that the City Mayor liquidate the cash advance within the period provided under Joint Circular No. 2015-01.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant agreed with the observation and assured compliance. Photocopy of Disbursement Voucher No. 2654-19-03 dated March 25, 2019 was submitted pertaining to the payment of Confidential expenses for the period March to May 2019 amounting to P7,600,000.00. The cash advance voucher together with its supporting documents were submitted to the Audit Team on March 27, 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require: a) Cashier to accept only valid Official Receipt as evidence of receipt of check/payment for the procurement of security services from the City for all transactions in accordance with the BIR regulations and provide justification in writing on the submitted invalid ORs/provisional receipts for the above transactions; b) BAC/TWG Secretariat to properly plan and consolidate the bidding for security services and properly plan the procurement to conform with the documentary requirements in the conduct of public bidding; c) Accountant to submit the supporting documents to prevent issuance of Notice of Suspension/Disallowance; and d) Employee in-charge in the internal audit under the Accounting Office, to exercise utmost diligence in processing claims and be properly oriented with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001.
observations.managementAction: According to the City Treasurer in his letter dated May 9, 2018, they noted the observation and demanded from the St. Vicentinian Security Services Inc. the official receipts and submitted the copies to the Audit Team on May 11, 2018. The procurement for security services in CY 2018 remain not consolidated but is now conforming to the documentary requirements in the conduct of public bidding.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require: a) Project Management Office (PMO) or the concerned end-users and the BAC to conduct market research and gather information about the products or services, its specifications and prices as inputs to the ABC; b) the GSO and BAC Secretariat to ensure that the ABCs of items for bid are based from careful determination of price reasonableness as circumstances warrant; c) BAC to set precise, complete and clear technical specifications; d) BAC to include in the ABC/PR/PO and in the contract agreement the detailed breakdown of the contract cost; e) BAC to properly evaluate the technical specifications submitted by bidders; f) members of the BAC to observe diligence; g) concerned officials to submit written explanations/comments on the noted deficiencies; h) BAC Secretariat, in coordination with the City Accountant, to submit the required supporting documents for audit purposes; and i) City Accountant to check thoroughly the completeness of supporting documents before processing and approving the payment of said disbursements/claims.
observations.managementAction: The City Government discontinued the procurement of janitorial services in CY 2018. The revised APP of the City Government for the period July to December 2018 conformed with the aforementioned regulation. The revised APP contains provision for Small Value Procurement for foreseeable emergencies.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Local Development Council (LDC) to strictly comply with the guidelines in the DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 on the appropriation and optimum utilization of the 20 per cent Development Fund on projects that partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures. Ensure that only capital expenditures are charged to the 20 per cent Development Fund.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2018, the LDRRMF was utilized in accordance with the City’s LDRRMFIP and programs implemented were projected to strengthen the City’s capacity for disaster reduction and management. The City complied with the guidelines and ensured that capital expenditures are charged to the 20 per cent Development Fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the members of the Sangguniang Panlungsod who represented the City in the MOA with grantees of Scholarship Program to desist from entering into such agreements in behalf of the City for lack of power and authority and that the stoppage of granting scholarship/financial assistance to prestigious/private universities and give priority to the less privileged students. (Note: recommendation text refers to scholarships in original table; for this observation on barangay allowances the recommendation is for submission of legal basis and cessation.)
observations.managementAction: The City official forwarded his reply letter that the City Government is clothed with authority to provide additional incentives to barangay functionaries and that such allowance when added to their present honoraria is still within the allowable adjustment of honorarium rates as per Local Government Code. He claimed the additional allowance has no legal basis and should be stopped. (Note: management comment in the table indicates the City Mayor provided justification.)

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the Disaster Risk and Reduction Office along with the other implementing offices to closely monitor the undertaking of the program in the said plan and ensure that projects, programs and activities envisioned to strengthen the City’s capacity for risk reduction and management be fully implemented.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2018, the LDRRMF was utilized in accordance with the City’s LDRRMFIP and programs implemented were projected to strengthen the City’s capacity for disaster reduction and management.
