observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require: a) formulate the City’s APP in accordance with Section 7 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; b) ensure that all the necessary information for its relevance and usefulness be contained in the APP; c) submit the duly approved copy of APP to the GPPB and posted in accordance with EO No. 662, series of 2007, as amended; d) attach the authenticated photocopy of the APP to the disbursement voucher for the payment of all types of procurement; and e) include in the APP, provisions for foreseeable emergencies based on historical records as well as the manner of procurement thereof.
observations.managementAction: The revised APP of the City Government for the period July to December 2018, after the issuance of audit report, conformed with the aforementioned regulation. The revised APP contained the necessary information and provided for Small Value Procurement for foreseeable emergencies. The revised APP is now being attached to every disbursement voucher as documentary requirement.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the Management follow strictly the above provisions of law on all of the submission of the AAPSI within the specified period to give the Auditor enough time to review, monitor and evaluate the action taken by the management on the recommendations.
observations.managementAction: The City submitted its AAPSI on October 15, 2018.
observations.reason: Not all the AAPSI for CY 2017 recommendations were submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Engineer to submit to COA the list of all ongoing government projects/programs/activities and those that are for implementation during the year, as well as information that the appropriate signboards and/or public notices have been already posted in accordance with COA Circular No. 2013-004 within the prescribed period.
observations.managementAction: The City Engineering Office submitted the list of all ongoing government projects. Information that appropriate signboards and/or public notices have been posted in accordance with COA Circular No. 2013-004 within the prescribed period were provided.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the General Services Officer to submit the Request for Inspection/Notice of Deliveries together with copies of the Acceptance and Inspection Report (AIR) to the Audit Team within 24 hours after acceptance and inspection of all deliveries by the Management.
observations.managementAction: The required Request for Inspection/Notice of Deliveries together with copies of the Acceptance and Inspection Report (AIR) were furnished to the Audit Team within 24 hours after acceptance and inspection of all deliveries.
observations.reason: Not all Request for Inspection/Notice of Delivery and Acceptance and Inspection Reports were submitted within the prescribed period; after the issuance of the AOM, the GSO started complying but sometimes still incur delay in the submission.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the procuring entity headed by the City Mayor and members of BAC and TWG, City Budget Officer, OIC-City Accountant submit explanations/justifications why the Purchase of Medical Equipment should not be disallowed in audit given absence of approved budget at start of procurement and non-availability of funds when contracts were entered, and to address deficiencies noted by COA Technical Audit Specialists.
observations.managementAction: On March 15, 2018, the Audit Team was furnished an Appeal Memorandum of the City on Notice of Disallowance Nos. 17-001-100-(16) dated November 2, 2017 to November 7, 2017 duly received by persons liable from January 9 to March 5, 2018. A separate Appeal Memorandum was filed by Hon. Leonilo A. Catipon, City Councilor on March 16, 2018. City officials were to address COA Technical Audit Specialists’ findings.
observations.reason: Awaiting result of Appeal Memorandum filed to the Regional Office.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the procuring entity headed by the City Mayor, the City Budget Officer, OIC-City Engineer and the OIC-City Accountant submit explanations/justifications why the Construction of Lipa Public Market Parking Building should not be disallowed in audit, in view of non-availability of funds when contract was entered into and non-compliance with PD No. 1445 provisions. Also, require City Engineering personnel who prepared and recommended approval of extension of contract time to submit explanations/justifications.
observations.managementAction: On March 15, 2018, the Audit Team was furnished an Appeal Memorandum of the City on Notice of Disallowance No. 17-001-100-(16) dated November 2, 2017 duly received by persons liable from January 9 to March 5, 2018. A separate Appeal Memorandum was filed by Hon. Leonilo A. Catipon on March 16, 2018.
observations.reason: Awaiting result of Appeal Memorandum filed to the Regional Office.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor stop immediately the approval for payment of unpaid prior year’s expenses charged against the appropriation of current year pursuant to Sections 336 and 350 of RA No. 7160. Require the City Budget Officer and City Accountant to strictly comply with Sections 336 and 350 to ensure funds are exclusively used for authorized purposes and to prevent unauthorized utilization of funds. Officials who caused approval/payment are jointly and severally liable for any audit disallowances.
observations.managementAction: On March 15, 2018, the Audit Team was furnished an Appeal Memorandum of the City on Notice of Disallowance Nos. 17-006-100-(16) to 17-042-200-(16) dated November 2, 2017 to November 30, 2017 duly received by persons liable from January 9 to March 30, 2018. The LGU ensured that expenditures charged against SEF will be in accordance with Section 272 of RA No. 7160 and related guidelines.
observations.reason: Awaiting result of Appeal Memorandum filed to the Regional Office.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor require the:
a) General Services Officer (GSO) to prepare and maintain Road Networks
property cards;
b) City Engineer to provide the City Accountant and the GSO with the
complete description and cost segregation of road components for road
projects; and
c) City Accountant to prepare and maintain property ledger cards for Local
Road Networks and disclose the information for the said property in the
Notes to Financial Statements in accordance with the prescribed format as
provided under COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 25, 2015.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:
a) Inventory Committee to start the physical count of all Inventory items for
General Fund (GF) and Special Education Fund (SEF) at an earlier date or
before the end of each prescribed period to give the members an ample time
to prepare the RPCI in proper form by type and submit the same to the
Auditor’s Office with the certification and approval of the Agency Head not
later than July 31 and January 31 of each year;
b) City Veterinary Office and General Services Office to coordinate with the
Accounting Office for the proper reclassification of the Veterinary Supplies
and Medicines account;
c) Accounting Unit and the GSO to maintain supplies ledger cards and stock
cards, respectively, for each inventory item/stock;
d) Accounting Unit to take up all purchases of items to Inventory account and
record the issuance based on Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued
(SSMI) only;
e) Accountant and the GSO to conduct periodic reconciliation of their records
and prepare adjustment of the affected accounts, if necessary; and
f) Community Social Welfare and Development Office/City Accounting Office
to submit an explanation on why there was an inventory for Welfare Goods
for Distribution when there was no known calamity that struck the City or
68
Sangguniang Panlungsod Resolution declaring the City in the State of
Calamity for CY 2017.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant and the
City Treasurer to ensure that all cash advances are supported with duly approved
payrolls and disbursement vouchers with their net payments as basis for drawing
cash advances in accordance with Section 49 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I
and COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 to avoid possible loss or
misuse of government funds.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to see to
it that all valid/lawful obligations are properly taken up in the accounts of the year
they were incurred, pursuant to Section 112 of PD No. 1445 and Section 4(a) of the
MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct:
a) the concerned officials to consolidate in one office the handling and
selection of City scholars to avoid the availment of more than one
scholarship and to conceptualize one Scholarship Program with rigid
rules and regulations that will cater all the poor and deserving students of
the City to ensure the proper utilization of the scarce resources of the
73
government;
b) the concerned officials to evaluate the scholarship programs and
determine the students who have more than one scholarship and to
require the students to refund the amount received for other scholarship
assistance;
c) the Accounting Office to use the Scholarship account instead of Donation
account in recording transactions pertaining to the grant of educational
assistance;
d) the respective Office to transfer the preparation of payroll for Sagip
Eskwela and MAS Educational Financial Assistance Program to the
Accounting Office to adhere to sound internal control;
e) the concerned Office to revisit the Implementing Guidelines and Work
Program for Sagip Eskwela and make necessary amendment on the
amount being granted;
f) the City Community Affairs Office to submit to the Audit Team the
documents to support the grant of MAS College Scholarship for
Indigents;
g) the concerned office to refrain from paying financial assistance to post
graduate students and other students who can afford to support their
studies, instead focus on those less privileged who cannot even attend to
high school or college level;
h) the concerned Office to revisit the guidelines set for the grant of Financial
Assistance to College Students which stated that it can be granted to
students even with failing grades or dropped subjects, provided the
beneficiaries are in school at the current semester;
i) the members of the Sangguniang Panlungsod who represented the City in
the MOA with grantees of Scholarship Program to desist from entering
into such agreements in behalf of the City for lack of power and
authority; and
j) the stoppage of granting scholarship/financial assistance to students
enrolled in the Prestigious/Private Universities/Colleges and give priority
to the less privileged students.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the BAC and TWG to:
a) explain in writing why they shall not be held liable for their failure to
conduct thorough evaluation of the submitted Tax Clearance Certificate and
annual income tax returns of the bidders during post-qualification
proceedings;
b) submit their basis for rating the legal, technical and financial documents
submitted by bidders as “Passed” during the preliminary evaluation and post-
qualification considering that they appeared to be ineligible but were awarded a
contract, otherwise the transactions will be disallowed in audit;
c) be more cautious in evaluating the legal, technical and financial documents
submitted by bidders during preliminary evaluation and post-qualification
before recommending the award of the contract; and
d) impose administrative sanctions/penalties to the contractors/suppliers for
the submission of eligibility requirements containing false information or
falsified documents.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:
a) City Accountant, City Budget Officer, and other local officials concerned to
strictly observe Item 6.1.5 of the Joint Circular wherein cash advances
should be limited to three month requirements only;
b) City Accountant to submit to the Audit Team, for review and verification,
the disbursement voucher for the grant of cash advance for confidential
expense, together with all the required and proper supporting documents,
within seven days upon release of checks, pursuant to the above regulation;
c) City Budget Officer to ensure that appropriation for Confidential Expenses
should be stated as such and not for Confidential/Intelligence Expense to
avoid confusion, considering that LGUs are limited only to Confidential Fund
and therefore their appropriations should only be for Confidential Expenses in
compliance with Joint Circular No. 2015-01;
d) City Accountant to provide clarifications on the period covered of the cash
78
advance drawn and the reason thereof; and
e) City Accountant, City Budget Officer, and other local officials concerned to
process and pay claims with complete documentation only.
We further recommended that the City Mayor liquidate the cash advance for
Confidential Expenses within the period provided under COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-
DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015.

We recommended that the City of Lipa discontinue the purchase of
accountable forms from RFI and strictly comply with Office of the President of the
Philippines Memorandum Circular No. 180, s. 2009 and DILG Memorandum
Circular No. 2012-32 dated February 15, 2012, and GPPB Resolution Nos. 05-2010
dated October 29, 2010; 04-2011 dated February 25, 2011; 08-2012 dated April 27,
2012 and 24-2012 dated November 23, 2012.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:
a) Cashier to accept only valid Official Receipt as evidence of payment by the City
for all transactions in accordance with the BIR regulations and provide
justification in writing on submitted invalid ORs/provisional receipts for the
above transactions;
b) BAC/TWG Secretariat to properly plan and consolidate the bidding for security
services and conform with the procedures and documentary requirements
necessary in all conduct of public bidding from the preparation of bidding
documents up to the awarding and implementation of contracts;
c) Accountant to submit the required supporting documents to prevent the
issuance of Notice of Suspensions/Disallowance, and to see to it that claims
should be supported with complete documentations to establish their
validity, legality and correctness of the claims; and
d) Employee in-charge in the internal audit under the Accounting Office, to
exercise utmost diligence in processing claims. He/She should be properly
oriented with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001 relative to documentary
requirements for common government transactions.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:
a) Project Management Office (PMO) or concerned end-users and the BAC to
conduct market research and to gather information about the products or
services, its specifications and prices, among others, as inputs to the ABC.
Support the preparation of ABC with proof of canvass conducted;
b) the GSO and BAC Secretariat to ensure that the ABCs of items for bid are
based from careful determination of price reasonableness as circumstances
warrant, update the prices of items in the ABC before the conduct of actual
bidding/procurement;
c) BAC to set a precise, complete and clear technical specifications (e.g.
delivery schedule, manpower requirements, supplies and materials, etc.), a clear
statement of the required standards of workmanship, materials, performance of
the goods and services to be procured;
d) BAC to include in the ABC/PR/PO and in the contract agreement the detailed
breakdown of the contract cost, and the direct and indirect cost such as janitorial
supplies and materials to be provided by the contractor as to kind, quantity and
amount;
e) BAC to properly evaluate the technical specifications submitted by bidders
as contained in the bid proposal and conform with the procedures and
documentary requirements necessary in all conduct of public bidding from
the preparation of bidding documents up to the awarding and
implementation of the contract;
f) members of the BAC to observe diligence in the conduct of eligibility
checking and post-qualification of the LCB to ensure that the City transacts
businesses with bonafide and legitimate suppliers only;
g) concerned officials to submit written explanations/comments on the noted
93
deficiencies;
h) BAC Secretariat in coordination with the City Accountant, to submit the
required supporting documents for audit purposes and to prevent the
issuance of Notice of Suspension/Disallowance; and
i) City Accountant to check thoroughly the completeness of supporting
documents before processing and approving the payment of said
disbursements/claims.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Local Development Council
(LDC) to strictly comply with the guidelines in the DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1
dated February 22, 2017, on the appropriation and optimum utilization of the 20%
Development Fund on projects that partake the nature of investment or capital
expenditures.

Ensure that only capital expenditures are charged to the 20% Development
Fund.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City official concerned to
provide/submit legal basis on the grant of additional allowance/honorarium to the
aforecited barangay employees, otherwise, these will be disallowed in audit being
considered irregular expenditures.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
a) require the City Accountant to withheld retention money in an amount
equivalent to at least one percent (1%) of every progress payment, or a
special bank guarantee equivalent to at least one percent (1%) of the total
contract price. The said amounts shall only be released after the lapse of the
warranty period or, in the case of Expendable Supplies, after consumption
thereof; and
b) instruct the BAC to submit the above documents for further evaluation of the
transaction. Strictly adhere with the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and other
related rules and regulations especially on the completeness of documents in
each stage of the bidding/procurement process.

We recommended that the Disaster Risk and Reduction Office along with the other
implementing offices such as General Services, Engineering and Welfare and
Development to closely monitor the undertaking of the proposed projects
funded out of LDRRMF so that the fund utilization be aligned with the City’s Local
Disaster Risk Reduction Management Investment Plan (LDRRMIP) and the
Projects, Programs and Activities envisioned to strengthen the City’s capacity for
risk reduction and management be fully implemented.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the General Services Officer,
BAC Secretariat and all the Department Heads to:
a) formulate the City’s APP in accordance with Section 7 of the Revised IRR of
RA No. 9184;
b) ensure that all the necessary information required in the APP and its
amendments are reflected therein for its continuous relevance and usefulness
as a planning and control tool;
c) submit the duly approved copy of APP to the GPPB in accordance with
Section 1(a) of Executive Order No. 662, series of 2007, as amended;
d) attach the authenticated photo copy of the APP to the disbursement vouchers for
the payment of all types of procurement, in compliance to COA Circular 2012-
001 dated June 14, 2012; and
e) include in the APP, provisions for foreseeable emergencies based on
historical records as well as the manner of procurement thereof.

We recommended that the Management follow strictly the above provisions
of law on the submission of the Agency Action Plan and Status of Implementation
(AAPSI) within the specified period to give enough time for the Auditor to review,
monitor and evaluate the action taken by the management on the audit
recommendations.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Engineer to submit to COA
the list of all ongoing government projects/programs/activities and those that are for
implementation during the year, as well as information that the appropriate
signboards and/or public notices have been already posted in accordance with COA
Circular No. 2013-004 within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the General Services Officer
to submit the Request for Inspection/Notice of Deliveries together with copies of the
Acceptance and Inspection Report (AIR) to the Audit Team within 24 hours after
acceptance and inspection of all the deliveries by the Management.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City GSO to cause the immediate submission to the Audit Team of the RPCPPE of the City. However, any proof of reconciliation between the records of CGSO and Accounting Office was not furnished to the Audit Team.
observations.managementAction: The City GSO submitted to the Audit Team the RPCPPE on April 25 and May 30, 2017, as well as their revised report on Infrastructure Projects, Buildings and Other Structures owned by the City. However, any proof of reconciliation between the records of CGSO and Accounting Office was not furnished to the Audit Team.
observations.reason: No reason provided

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require both the Officer-in-Charge of the City Accountant and the City General Office to reconcile records of motor vehicles being maintained by their offices for fair presentation of Motor Vehicles account in the City’s financial statements. Accordingly, record the same in the books of accounts considering that these vehicles are in existence and being used in the operation of the City Government; cause the immediate disposal of all unserviceable/junked vehicles through proper disposal procedures of government property pursuant to COA Circular No. 92-386; make the necessary adjustment on the Report of Inventory of Motor Vehicles of the acquisition costs of eight vehicles with over/understatement of P371,768.31; cause the marking of every motor vehicles owned by the City Government in accordance with COA Circular No. 75-6; and ensure that all government vehicles carry the LTO issued plate number in its appropriate place for easy identification.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer’s Office secured an authorization from the Sangguniang Panlungsod as part of actions regarding their disposal. The CGSO initiated the necessary proceedings in connection with the disposal of 75 unserviceable/junked vehicles and the required documents for the same have been submitted to the Audit Team on June 29, 2017 and September 8, 2017. The CGSO submitted a single consolidated report regardless of source of funds. The Accounting Department followed the required asset account booking. The LGU claimed that all vehicles are now marked with the Official Seal of Lipa City, and that all owned vehicles are now carrying the Official LTO license plates. The LGU also claimed some vehicles were donated to national government offices.
observations.reason: Some of the LGU owned vehicles are not carrying the Official LTO license plates and had not yet been marked with the Official Seal of Lipa City, for proper identification and monitoring.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer and the City Veterinary offices to reconcile the differences noted in order to arrive at the correct balances of accounts at any given period. Likewise, require all accountable officers of inventory items to maintain complete and updated inventory ledger cards and inventory stock cards per class/category of property as a means of control, and to protect the integrity of information being reported in the financial statements of the City Government.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer’s Office presented the justification for the unreconciled balances in their account in their management comment dated April 17, 2017. The City Accounting Office is adopting the chart of accounts and requested City Veterinary Office and General Services Office to adopt the same account title in their reports. The Accounting Department maintains General and Subsidiary Ledgers via E-NGAS; a compilation of SL for inventories was submitted to COA on May 7, 2018. A directive will be issued to adopt Requisition and Issue Slip and prepare monthly consolidations (SSMI). The City Accounting Office shall work with CGSO to schedule and implement periodic reconciliations. The City Accounting Office processed disbursement voucher for the purchase of welfare goods for distribution and maintains that stockpiling of basic emergency supplies may be procured by CSWDO.
observations.reason: Some inventory ledger cards per class/category of property are still not maintained.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the OIC-City Accountant and the City Treasurer to make proper representation with the Philippine Veterans Bank and the Philippine National Bank in order to reconcile the outstanding records of accounts and the total payments made to principal amounts and interest expenses, for fair presentation of the affected accounts in the financial statements of the City of Lipa.
observations.managementAction: The incumbent City Accountant reasoned that the differences noted was due to the fact that the City’s payments of loan amortization for the month of December 2016 were recorded only by both banks in January 2017, thus the statement issued by both banks to the Audit Team did not include the amounts paid by the City Government. In the explanations made by the City Administrator on June 14, 2017, the discrepancies noted by the Audit Team have been given detailed explanations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Treasurer to transfer to current account the total time deposits of P240,000,000.00, and ensure safety of the funds from unforeseen events, and that P20,000,000.00 included in the P240,000,000.00 time deposit was returned to the current account per JEV No. 2017-04-000154 dated April 17, 2017.
observations.managementAction: The CTO secured an authorization from the Sangguniang Panlungsod through SP Resolution No. 79, dated April 4, 2017 authorizing the City Treasurer to invest the City Government’s idle funds in term deposit or investment savings account with any government financial institution for a period of thirty (30) days. The P20,000,000.00 was returned to the current account per JEV dated April 17, 2017.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor stop immediately the grant of excessive cash advances to one Disbursing Officer to avoid possible loss or improper utilization of City funds. Require both the City Treasurer and the OIC-City Accountant to enforce rules and regulations on the granting and utilization of cash advance pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997: limit cash advances for payroll to the net amount of the payrolls; set fixed cash advance amounts for recurring expenses not exceeding P15,000 per transaction; separate cash advances for payroll and for operating expenses; and require RATA payments to be coursed through ATM.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer noted the observation, coordinated with concerned implementing offices for guidance. A separate disbursing officer was designated for cash advances for special projects – MAS educational financial assistance for senior high school, college students, SAGIP Eskwela and others. Implemented verbal extensions for unclaimed benefits and plan to shorten to two days and immediately refund excess. For payroll of Job Order and Casual employees, a cut-off period will be implemented. The LGU uses separate cash advances for each fund and payments of recurring operating expenses follow recommendations. RATA are now being paid through ATM.
observations.reason: Recommendation was not implemented within the period.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor comply with JC No. 2015-01 of COA, DBM, DILG, GCG and DND: require the OIC-City Accountant, City Budget Officer, and other local officials to submit to the Audit Team the DV for grant of cash advance for confidential expense, with required supporting documents, within seven days upon release; use the term Confidential Fund instead of Intelligence Fund; observe Section 6.1.5 of the Joint Circular limiting cash advances to three months; and process/pay claims with complete documentation only.
observations.managementAction: The City Mayor, City Accountant, City Budget Officer and other officials promised to comply. The City stopped use of the term ‘Intelligence Fund’ and adopted ‘Confidential Funds’. The LGU did not fully comply with Section 6.1.5 of the Joint Circular; disbursement voucher and supporting documents were submitted to the Audit Team on March 7, 2017 (47 days after release) and liquidation documents were submitted to COA Chairman on January 24, 2018. The LGU will process and pay claims only with complete documentation.
observations.reason: Not within prescribed period; still non-compliant with Section 6.1.5.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the Local Development Council to ensure that only programs and projects geared towards socio-economic development and environmental management that are capital in nature be charged to the 20% Development Fund, and to ensure compliance with the law and implementing regulations. Ensure that only capital expenditures are charged to the 20% Development Fund.
observations.managementAction: In compliance for CY 2017, the LGU appropriated the costs of garbage hauling and disposal in 2018 under General Fund MOOE. Wages of Job Orders and procurement of various supplies/services for January-June 2017 were not charged to 20% DF in CY 2018. The City stopped charging certain items to the 20% DF starting July 2017 and planned to stop others in CY 2018.
observations.reason: Full compliance pending; some reallocations were made in CY 2018.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Administrator to ensure strict compliance with EO No. 51 whereby the NFA shall be the supplier unless a waiver is issued; request required waiver from NFA for procurement of rice; and submit necessary documents to substantiate disaster mitigation activities actually occurred, otherwise distribution will be considered irregular expenditures. Also require CDRRMO to strictly follow projects/activities chargeable against LDRRMF as per JMC No. 2013-1.
observations.managementAction: The City Government furnished the Audit Team a copy of a certification from the SP Secretary that the City Mayor has requested authority to enter into an MOA with the NFA; the LGU also submitted two certifications: (1) dated Sept 18, 2017 certifying NFA decision to place on standby supply of rice to certain programs pending release of selling price bulletin; and (2) dated Feb 1, 2018 certifying unavailability of premium/fancy rice variety. The LGU claims prior audit recommendations remain not complied with but provided the above certifications.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct concerned officials to submit the detailed breakdown of the ABC and contract amount showing derivation including number of paleros/laborers/crew, prices/costs of spare parts, machine shop costs and other maintenance parts to determine reasonableness of the contract price. observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City GSO to cause the
immediate submission to the Audit Team of the RPCPPE of the City
Government so as to determine the existence of these properties, and for her to
reconcile the records maintained by her Office with the City Accounting Office
so that the balances of PPE accounts being presented in the financial statements
of the City Government would attain fairness of presentation as of year-end.

We recommended that the City Mayor require both the Officer-in-
Charge of the Offices of the City Accountant and the City General Services to
reconcile the records of motor vehicles being maintained by their offices for fair
presentation of Motor Vehicles account in the City’s financial statements. Both
officials should act on the following:

* Locate the 122 units of vehicles that were not accounted during the physical
inventory through retrieval of all documents related to their acquisition,
including copies of Memorandum Receipts/Acknowledgement Receipt for
Equipment/Property Acknowledgement Receipt, which contained the names
of persons to whom said vehicles had been entrusted to, and make them
accountable for the loss, if any. If proven futile, proper procedures on
dropping from the books of accounts may be an option;

* Locate the supporting documents of 48 units of vehicles worth
P17,995,285.00 which are not recorded in the books of the City Government,
but were included in the Report of Inventory of Motor Vehicles maintained
by the GSO. Accordingly, record the same in the books of accounts
considering that these vehicles are in existence and being used in the
operation of the City Government;

* Cause the immediate disposal of all unserviceable/junked vehicles through
proper disposal procedures of government property pursuant to COA
Circular No. 92-386. This action could lead to recovery of whatever salvage
value that these properties still carry for the best interest of the City
Government;

* Make the necessary adjustment on the Report of Inventory of Motor
Vehicles of the acquisition costs of eight vehicles with over/understatement of
P371,768.31:

¢ Cause the marking of every motor vehicles owned by the City Government in
accordance with COA Circular No. 75-6; and

* Ensure that all government vehicles earry the LTO issued plate number in its
appropriate place for easy identification and monitoring.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the OIC-City Accountant
and the corresponding heads of the Offices of Treasury and Veterinary to
reconcile the differences noted in order to arrive at the correct balances of
accounts at any given period. Likewise, require all accountable officers of
inventory items to maintain complete and updated inventory ledger cards and
inventory stock cards per class/category of property as a means of control, and

to protect the integrity of information being reported in the financial statements
of the City Government.

‘We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the OIC-City Accountant
and the City Treasurer to make proper representation with the Philippine
Veterans Bank and the Philippine National Bank in order to reconcile the
difference of £5,087,397.28 from the recorded outstanding loan obligations, and
the total payments made to principal amounts and interest expenses, for fair
presentation of the affected accounts in the financial statements of the City of
Lipa as of December 31, 2016.

‘We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Treasurer to
transfer to current account the total time deposits of £240,000,000.00, which the
City Government of Lipa has been maintaining with the Development Bank of
the Philippines, Lipa City Branch since March 2014, due to absence of authority
from the SP and to ensure safety of the funds from unforeseen events, which
may be brought by uncertainty in the banking sector.

We recommended that the City Mayor stop immediately the grant of
excessive cash advances to one Disbursing Officer to avoid possible loss or
improper utilization of City funds. Likewise, require both the City Treasurer
and the OIC-City Accountant to enforce strictly, the rules and regulations on the
granting and utilization of cash advance, pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002
dated February 10, 1997, as follows:

¢ Limit the amount of cash advances for Payroll Fund to the net amount of the
payrolls for a particular period only:

¢ Set a fixed amount of cash advance for the payment of recurring operating
expenses only, where each transaction should not exceed P15,000,00.
Replenishment of the fund should be made when disbursements reached
75% to ensure availability of sufficient funds for other recurring obligations;

* Have a separate cash advance for Payroll Fund, and another cash advance
for the payment of recurring operating expenses, and at no instance should
operating expense be charged to the cash advance for Payroll Fund, and
payroll to the cash advance for operating expenses. Petty operating expenses
should be limited only to recurring expenses; and

* Require that payment of RATA to City Officials be coursed thru the
Automatic Teller Machine (ATM) considering that this expenditure is
recurring and attached to the position of the personnel concerned, thus
saving time, effort, and manpower resources.

We recommended that the City Mayor strictly comply with the provisions
of Joint Circular No. 2015-01 of the COA, DBM, DILG, Governance
Commission of the GOCC, and DND on the use of Confidential Funds. He
should require the OIC-City Accountant, City Budget Officer, and other local
officials concerned to:

* submit to the Audit Team, for review and verification, the disbursement
voucher for the grant of cash advance for confidential expense, together
with all the required and proper supporting documents, within the
prescribed period pursuant to the above regulation;

* use the term Confidential Fund instead of Intelligence Fund when
appropriating funds for the confidential expenses under the POP of the
City, as appropriate only for local government units;

* strictly observe Section 6.1.5 of the Joint Circular, where cash advances
should be limited to three months, and any additional amount should
only be granted after the liquidation of previous cash advances; and

e process and pay claims with complete documentation only.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the Local Development
Council to ensure that only programs and projects geared towards the
attainment of socio-economic development and environmental management
outcomes that partake the pature of investment or capital expenditures, as
mandated by the law and its implementing rules and regulations, will be given
allocation under the 20% Development Fund to ensure the full realization of the
purpose for which the Fund was created.

We recommended that the procuring entity headed by the City Mayor
and the members of the Bids and Awards Committee and the Technical
Working Group, the City Budget Officer, City Treasurer and the City
Accountant submit explanations/justifications why the Purchase of Medical
Equipment of P169,481,107.17 should not be disallowed in audit, in view of the
absence of approved budget for the contract at the start of the procurement
process and the non-availability of funds when contracts were entered into on
June 23, 2014, as required under Sections 85 and 86 of PD No. 1445, where non-
compliance, as provided under Section 87 of PD No. 1445, means that contracts
entered into shall be void and the officer or officers entering into the contract
shall be liable to the government or other contracting party for any consequent
damage to the same extent as if the transaction had been wholly between private
parties.

We likewise recommended that the honorable members of the
Sangguniang Panlungsod submit explanation/justification why the Supplemental
Budget was approved thru SP Ordinance No. 2014-08 and SP Special Ordinance
No. 158-2015 even though the said purchase of medical equipment was not
included in the approved Annual Procurement Plan of the City for CY 2014,

Further, require the City General Services Officer and the Chief of
Hospital of Ospital ng Lipa to address immediately the deficiencies noted by the
COA Technical Audit Specialists as a result of the Technical Evaluation Report
issued by them.

We recommended that the procuring entity headed by the City Mayor,
the City Budget Officer, OIC-City Engineer, and the OIC-City Accountant
submit explanations/justifications why the Construction of Lipa Public Market
Parking Building of P296,788,888.88 should not be disallowed in audit, in view
of the non-availability of funds when contract was entered into on May 8, 2014,
as required under Section 86 of PD No. 1445, where non-compliance, as
provided under Section 87 of PD No. 1445, means that contracts entered into
shall be void and the officer or officers entering into the contract shall be liable
to the government or other contracting party for any consequent damage to the
same extent as if the transaction had been wholly between private parties.

We likewise recommended that the City Engineering personnel who
prepared and recommended to the City Mayor the approval of extension of
contract time to 719 calendar days on the account of unfavorable weather
condition submit explanation/justification on the actions taken by them, which
was contrary to Section 10.2 of Annex “E” of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184,

We recommended that the City Mayor stop immediately the approval for
payment of any unpaid prior year's expenses charged against the appropriation
of current year pursuant to the provisions of Sections 336 and 350 of the RA No.
7160.

Instruct the City Budget Officer and the City Accountant to strictly
comply with the aforecited provisions of Sections 336 and 350 of RA No. 7160 to
ensure that the funds are exclusively used for the purpose for which they were
appropriated, and to prevent unauthorized utilization of funds.

Officials who caused the approval and payment of such kind of
transactions are jointly and severally liable for any audit disallowances in view
of the violations of existing rules and regulations.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the Head of the City
Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council (CDRRMC) to stop the
procurement and distribution of rice instead of cash for labor in disaster
mitigation activity, and for food in disaster preparedness seminar as such
undertaking is not among the activities chargeable against the LDRRMF and
has no bearing with the real intent of LDRRMF.

Require the CDRRMC 1o strictly follow the projects and activities that
can be charged against the LDRRMF as prescribed in Joint Memorandum
Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013 of the NDRRMC, DBM and the
DILG to ensure that funds thereof are fully ntilized in accordance with the real
intention of setting up the Funds.

Meanwhile, require the CDRRMO to submit the necessary documents to
substantiate that disaster mitigation activities actually occurred, otherwise, the
distribution of 25,000 sacks of rice of P5,487,500.00 would be considered as
irregular expenditures against LDRRMEF. As to the procurement of 36,363 sacks
at a total cost of £7,981,678.50 for disaster preparedness seminar, it is already
considered as irregular expenditure.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Administrator to ensure the
City’s strict compliance with the provision of EO No. 51, whereby the NFA shall be
the supplier of rice that the government entity would need in connection with its
functions, unless a waiver is issued by the NFA.

Likewise, instruct the concerned official to submit to the Audit Team the
required waiver from the NFA for the procurement of rice for three consecutive
years since 2014, as compliance with previous years’ audit recommendation for
similar transactions, has remained not complied with by the City Government.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the officials concerned to
submit the detailed breakdown of the ABC and of the contract amount showing
in sufficient detail its derivation, including the number of
“paleros™/laborers/crew, prices/costs of spare parts, machine shop costs and
other maintenance parts used in the detailed breakdown of the contract amount
50 as to determine the reasonableness of the contract price of £23,458,905.69.

Likewise, require the concerned City officials to submit all the required
documents found lacking as support documents, and the necessary
clarifications/justifications for the deficiencies noted to substantiate the
payments made. Also, strict compliance with the procedures and documentary
requirements for government procurement prescribed in the aforecited laws,
rules and regulations should be adopted 50 as to ensure that all contracts entered
into by the City Government is valid and legal.

Further, require all city officials to process, approve and pay claims only with
complete documentation to protect the interest of the City Government.

We recommended that the City Mayor stop immediately the payment of
allowances to school teachers out of the appropriation for the SEF as such
expense is contrary to the provisions of Section 272 of RA No. 7160, and its IRR
as embodied under JC No. 01 s 1998 of the DBM, DECS, & DILG.

Consequently, require the concerned school teachers to refund the total
amount of $29,026,476.92 to the City Treasury so as to provide SEF sufficient
funds to finance the operation and maintenance of public schools in the City of
Lipa.

Likewise, require the Local School Board to ensure that expenditures
charged against the SEF include only those among the priority list of expenses to
be charged against SEF as provided under JC No. 01 s, 1998, thus ensuring the
welfare of school children and that SEF are being utilized only for the purpose
for which it was created.
If funds are available and the City Council approves, said allowances
may be charged against the General Fund so as to pursue continuously the City
Government’s assistance to all public school teachers.

We recommended that the City Mayor stop the grant of fixed monthly
Traveling Allowance and Taskforce Allowance to the members of PNP personnel
and some City Hall employees as such allowances are not covered by any specific
law authorizing the grant to government officials and employees. Hence, the
total amount of $2,204,000.00 as Traveling and Taskforce Allowances (o
members of PNP personnel and several City employees as Security Aides are
126
considered double compensation that is prohibited under Section 95 of RA No.
7160.

Consequently, require the refund of the total amount of P2.204,000.00
from the concerned personnel.

We recommended that the City Mayor stop giving approval on the
payment of allowances to national government officials and employees not
specifically included under Section 458(1)(vi) of RA No. 7160, LBC No. 62 dated
July 29, 1996, and DBM Compensation Policy Guidelines No, 98-1, to prevent
unnecessary disbursement of government funds.

Henceforth, require the refund of $2,967,700.00 from the concerned
national government employees considering that they are not among those
officials entitled to receive said allowances.

We recommended that the City Mayor stop approving the payment of
honorarium to members of the Board of Trustees of the ONL and KLL unless
sufficient legal basis for the grant of said honorarium, other than the
aforementioned Special Ordinances, has been submitted to the Audit Team for
further evaluation and analysis of the matter on hand. Otherwise, the payment of
said honoraria would be considered an audit disallowance.

We recommended that the City Mayor stop immediately the approval of
payments of honoraria to members of various executive committees considering
that membership to such committees do not entail them to receive honoraria,
and to discontinue granting thereof, pursuant to the cited provisions of
government laws, rules and regulations to avoid incurrence of irregular
expenditure.

Consequently, require the concerned City officials and employees to
refund the amount they received as honoraria as such payment is without legal
basis, hence, an irregular expenditure in the total amount of 21,875,177.50.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City's LDRRMO
and/or the City Accountant to regularly prepare a monthly Report on the
Utilization of the LDRRMF and submit the same to the Audit Team within the
period prescribed by the above regulation for immediate audit action so that
corresponding result may be brought to the attention of concerned officials the
soonest possible time for their immediate and appropriate action.

We recommended that the City Mayor avail all legal remedies to enforce
the collection of the outstanding balance of £1,432,733.18 out of the total audit
disallowances of £4,810,132.50 from all persons liable therefor, as appropriate
NDs and COA OE have been duly issued and receipted by them, so as to protect
the interest of the City Government.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the Chairman of the Bids
and Awards Committee of the City Government to cause the immediate
submission of contracts for infrastructure projects, including all documents
forming part thereof, to the Audit Team within five days upon perfection of said
contracts for the required review and analysis, so that any observation/s that
may be found in the course of analysis or evaluation of the subject documents, be
immediately relayed to the concerned City officials, and corresponding measures
be implemented for the best interest of the City Government.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the concerned city
officials to submit the corresponding Official Receipts of the payment made to
ULAP and LCP so as to substantiate the payments made and to avoid the
suspension of the aforementioned disbursements.
